stem: The matrix \( P = \begin{bmatrix} [[e11]] & [[e12]] \\ [[e21]] & [[e22]] \end{bmatrix} \) is idempotent (it satisfies \( P^{2} = P \)). What is the \( (1,2) \) entry (top-right entry) of \( P^{[[n]]} \)?
solution: Since \( P \) is idempotent, \( P^{2} = P \), and therefore \( P^{n} = P \) for every \( n \ge 1 \). So the \( (1,2) \) entry of \( P^{[[n]]} \) equals the \( (1,2) \) entry of \( P \), which is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the \( (1,1) \) entry of \( P \), not the \( (1,2) \) entry.
This is the \( (2,2) \) entry of \( P \), not the \( (1,2) \) entry.
This is the \( (2,1) \) entry of \( P \), not the \( (1,2) \) entry.
This is the trace of \( P \) (a projection's trace equals its rank), not the requested entry.
This is one more than the correct entry.
This is one less than the correct entry.
This is two more than the correct entry.
This is two less than the correct entry.

stem: The matrix \( P = \begin{bmatrix} [[e11]] & [[e12]] \\ [[e21]] & [[e22]] \end{bmatrix} \) is idempotent (it satisfies \( P^{2} = P \)) and is not the identity matrix. What is \( \det(P) \)?
solution: A nonzero idempotent matrix that is not the identity has eigenvalues only \( 0 \) and \( 1 \), with at least one eigenvalue equal to \( 0 \) (else \( P \) would be the identity). So \( \det(P) \), the product of its eigenvalues, is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the \( (1,1) \) entry of \( P \), not its determinant.
This is the \( (2,2) \) entry of \( P \), not its determinant.
The determinant of this idempotent matrix is not \( 1 \) (that would require \( P \) to be the identity).
A projection matrix's determinant cannot be negative; its eigenvalues are only \( 0 \) or \( 1 \).
This is two more than the correct determinant.
This is three less than the correct determinant.

stem: The \( 3\times 3 \) block-diagonal matrix \( P = \begin{bmatrix} [[e11]] & [[e12]] & 0 \\ [[e21]] & [[e22]] & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & [[e]] \end{bmatrix} \) is idempotent: its top-left \( 2\times2 \) block satisfies \( B^{2}=B \), and its \( (3,3) \) entry is \( 0 \) or \( 1 \). What is the rank of \( P \)?
solution: For an idempotent matrix, the rank equals the trace: the top-left block contributes rank (and trace) \( 1 \), and the \( (3,3) \) entry contributes \( [[e]] \) more. So \( \mathrm{rank}(P) = 1 + [[e]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: A nonzero idempotent matrix cannot have rank \( 0 \).
The matrix's top-left block alone already accounts for rank \( 1 \) with a possible \( 0 \) or \( 1 \) more, not a full rank of \( 3 \).
This is just the \( (3,3) \) entry alone, ignoring the rank-\( 1 \) contribution from the block.
This overcounts the block's contribution.
This is two more than the correct rank.
This is two less than the correct rank.

stem: The matrix \( P = \begin{bmatrix} [[e11]] & [[e12]] \\ [[e21]] & [[e22]] \end{bmatrix} \) is idempotent (\( P^{2}=P \)), so its complement \( Q = I - P \) is idempotent too. What is the \( (1,1) \) entry (top-left entry) of \( Q^{[[n]]} \)?
solution: Since \( Q = I - P \) is idempotent, \( Q^{n} = Q \) for every \( n \ge 1 \). Its \( (1,1) \) entry is \( 1 - ([[e11]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the \( (1,1) \) entry of \( P \) itself, not of its complement \( Q = I-P \).
This is the \( (1,2) \) entry of \( P \), not the \( (1,1) \) entry of \( Q \).
This is the \( (2,1) \) entry of \( P \), not the \( (1,1) \) entry of \( Q \).
This is one more than the correct entry.
This is one less than the correct entry.
This is two more than the correct entry.
This is two less than the correct entry.
